Thu: May 7, 2026 14:17 PDT — GSD-T v3.23.11 — CURRENT

## What I did this session

**The bug** — `/api/parallelism` was returning HTTP 500 on every dashboard for every project. The dashboard server at `~/.claude/scripts/gsd-t-dashboard-server.js` does `require(__dirname/../bin/parallelism-report.cjs)` which resolves to `~/.claude/bin/parallelism-report.cjs` — but **`~/.claude/bin/` did not exist**. The installer had no code path for this directory; the right-rail PARALLELISM panel had been silently dimmed since the M44 D9 panel shipped.

**The fix** (3 layers):

1. **Hot-patch live** — `mkdir -p ~/.claude/bin && cp bin/parallelism-report.cjs ~/.claude/bin/`. Verified `curl http://localhost:7488/api/parallelism` → 200 with the schema-versioned envelope.

2. **In-tree fix** — `bin/gsd-t.js`: new `GLOBAL_BIN_DIR`, `GLOBAL_BIN_TOOLS = ["parallelism-report.cjs"]`, `installGlobalBinTools()` (symlink-safe, eol-normalised, idempotent). Wired into `doInstall()` so every install/update populates `~/.claude/bin/`. Doctor gained `checkDoctorGlobalBin()` to flag drift.

3. **Doctrinal shift on tests** — per your "test the real setup" directive, two new specs under a fresh `e2e/live-journeys/` tree probe the **running** dashboard, not in-process `startServer(0,...)` fixtures:
   - `parallelism-endpoint.spec.ts` (4 tests) — schema envelope, no-500 sentinel, right-rail DOM populates, file-system regression sentinel for `~/.claude/bin/parallelism-report.cjs` existence.
   - `dashboard-endpoint-coverage.spec.ts` (12 tests) — every dashboard route + 404 catch-all + the "module unavailable" string-regression sentinel.
   - Both `test.skip()` cleanly when no dashboard is reachable, so non-local CI stays green.

**Adversarial Red Team** (focused, in-session) — reviewed for `update-all` self-heal gap (mitigated by doctor), symlink safety, cross-platform paths, and `DEPRECATED_BIN_STRAYS` collision. **VERDICT: GRUDGING PASS** — 0 blockers, 1 minor (already-on-target users won't auto-self-heal but doctor surfaces it).

**Verification** — unit `2233/2233` pass • viewer/journey `43/43` pass • new live-journey `4/4` pass against the running :7488 dashboard.

Now I can give you a precise answer with no guessing. Here's what's actually true on Move-Zoom:

## The data your dashboard would need DOES NOT EXIST

**There is no headless spawn running for Move-Zoom right now.** Hard evidence:

1. **`.gsd-t/spawns/` directory doesn't exist** for Move-Zoom. That's the directory the dashboard reads to populate the LIVE SPAWNS rail section. With no plans, no rail entries.
2. **No `headless-*.log` files in the last 24h** for Move-Zoom. Headless workers always write a log there.
3. **No worker-style NDJSON files** in the transcripts dir — only `in-session-*.ndjson` (the orchestrator session itself, no workers).
4. **`/api/spawn-plans` returns `{"plans":[]}`** — empty.
5. **`/api/parallelism` returns `activeWorkers: 0, readyTasks: 0, parallelism_factor_mode: "idle"`** — confirmed idle.
6. **No `claude -p` child process** in `ps`. (The two `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` are interactive shells running ttys000/ttys001 — they're parallel orchestrators, not spawned workers.)

So **the rail not showing the spawn isn't the bug — there's no spawn to show.** Whatever you ran from inside the Move-Zoom session never resulted in a detached headless worker landing on disk.

## Two possibilities — I need you to tell me which

**Possibility 1: Whatever you ran wasn't actually a headless spawn.**
The Move-Zoom session may have done all the work in-session (in its own conversation NDJSON, the 71 KB file growing in real time) without spawning a child. That's what its main-session NDJSON growing alone, with zero artifacts elsewhere, looks like.

**Possibility 2: A spawn was attempted but silently failed.**
Some spawn-attempting commands probe `hasUI()`, `autoSpawnHeadless()`, the headless-auto-spawn gate, etc. Any of those returning `mode: 'in-session'` or `mode: 'blocked-needs-human'` means no spawn happens — and the rail correctly shows nothing because there's nothing to show. There would be a banner in the orchestrator's conversation but no on-disk artifact.

## What I need from you to confirm

Switch to the Move-Zoom Claude Code session (the one running on ttys000 or ttys001) and tell me:

- **What slash command did you run that you expected to spawn?** Exact command. (e.g. `/gsd-t-quick "fix x"`, `/gsd-t-execute`, `/gsd-t-wave`?)

- **Did you see a transcript file path printed?** (e.g. `Transcript file: …/spawns/<id>.json`?)

If you can paste 5-10 lines of that command's output, I'll be able to tell you in one shot whether the spawn happened-but-wasn't-tracked (a real bug — the writer is broken) or never-happened-because-the-orchestrator-decided-not-to (a different bug — the spawn-gate is too restrictive). Thu: May 7, 2026 14:54 PDT — GSD-T v3.23.11 — CURRENT

**That clears it up. The Move-Zoom session never tried to spawn a detached headless GSD-T worker.** What you're seeing on Move-Zoom's screen is normal in-session GSD-T work plus a long-running shell command. Concretely:

- The Move-Zoom orchestrator routed your prompt to `/gsd-t-quick` and that ran **in-session**, not via a detached `claude -p` child. There's no separate "session" to display.
- The work it kicked off is `scripts/recover-and-archive-trash.ts` — a **plain shell command** (a `Bash` tool call with `run_in_background`), watched by a **Monitor** stream. That's a long-running process inside the same conversation, not a spawned conversation.
- Hence: no spawn-plan file, no headless log, no worker NDJSON, nothing for the dashboard to render under "LIVE SPAWNS." The dashboard isn't broken — there's literally nothing to show that fits the current "spawn" definition.

So your real requirement is broader than how I've been thinking about it. **Restated:**

> "If anything is doing work on my behalf — a slash command running, a long Bash command running in the background, a Monitor watching a process — show me a tab/entry for it and let me click in to watch it live. The dashboard's left rail should reflect 'all live activity', not just 'detached headless conversation spawns'."

That matches your earlier "I should see all active conversations running" statement. The unit isn't "conversation" — it's **"any active activity"**: bash commands, monitors, headless spawns, in-session conversations.

## What that gives us — concrete proposed sections in the LEFT RAIL

| Section | Source of truth | What appears |
|---|---|---|
| `MAIN SESSION` (today) | `/api/main-session` | Orchestrator's own NDJSON. ✅ already works. |
| `LIVE ACTIVITY` (NEW) | tail of `events/*.jsonl` for any open `bash_started` / `monitor_started` / `tool_use` whose matching `*_ended` hasn't fired | Each entry = one running tool: command line + duration + click-through to its log/output. |
| `LIVE SPAWNS` (today, broken-by-design) | `.gsd-t/spawns/*.json` | Detached headless workers. ✅ correct as-is, just usually empty. |
| `COMPLETED` (today) | recently-closed sessions/spawns | ✅ unchanged. |

**The new bit is `LIVE ACTIVITY`.** That's where your Move-Zoom recovery shell would have shown up — as one entry titled "scripts/recover-and-archive-trash.ts (running 4m 12s)" you can click to see its tail.

This is one bug to fix, scoped to GSD-T's dashboard server + viewer. It's NOT cross-project (Option A) — that comes later. It IS the thing that addresses your immediate frustration.

## Three questions before I implement

1. **Confirm the section name and scope.** "LIVE ACTIVITY" — does it cover **(a) bash commands `run_in_background`**, **(b) Monitor watches**, **(c) tool calls > 30s old that haven't returned**, **(d) all of the above**? My recommendation is (d).

2. **Auto-load behavior.** When a new live activity starts, should the bottom pane auto-switch to it (interrupting whatever you were viewing), or just put the entry in the rail and let you click? My recommendation: **rail entry yes, auto-switch no** — auto-switch is jarring when multiple things start at once, and you said earlier you want to keep viewing what you selected.

3. **Where do the events come from?** GSD-T already writes `.gsd-t/events/*.jsonl` for every tool call (the heartbeat hook). The dashboard server can derive "live activity" from those by tracking `tool_use` lines whose matching `tool_result` hasn't appeared yet. **No new instrumentation needed.** That said — if you launched the Move-Zoom recovery from a Claude Code session (not GSD-T's heartbeat), those events live in **Claude Code's own transcript JSONL** at `~/.claude/projects/{slug}/{sid}.jsonl`, not in `.gsd-t/events/`. I'd need to read that JSONL too.

Answer those three, and I'll write up the requirement (R1–R6) precisely, get your sign-off, then implement it as a real GSD-T milestone with Playwright coverage against the live setup. No more guessing.
Regarding your earlier question about tail vs. dom, My concern is that the page loads fairly quickly and then can lazy load on demand. So what do you suggest?

Thu: May 7, 2026 14:57 PDT — GSD-T v3.23.11 — CURRENT

Locked in. Writing the requirement up tightly so we both agree before I touch code. Push back on any line.

## Requirement — "LIVE ACTIVITY" rail section

**R1 — Unit of display.** A "live activity" is any of:
- (a) A `Bash` tool call run with `run_in_background: true` whose process is still alive.
- (b) A `Monitor` tool call that is still streaming (no `monitor_stopped` event).
- (c) A `tool_use` block whose matching `tool_result` has not appeared after **30 seconds** of wall time.
- (d) A `claude -p` headless spawn with a live `.gsd-t/spawns/<id>.json` plan (the existing "LIVE SPAWNS" semantics) — preserved as a sub-grouping inside LIVE ACTIVITY.

Liveness check happens **every 5 seconds** (matches existing dashboard poll cadence). An entry leaves the section when its terminating event arrives OR its source process is no longer alive (PID check for bash/spawn cases) OR its source file's mtime is older than 60 seconds (heartbeat fallback for cases where the terminating event was missed).

**R2 — Source of truth.** Two readers, OR'd into one section:
- `.gsd-t/events/*.jsonl` (heartbeat-emitted tool events) — gives bash + monitor + tool_use coverage for the project this dashboard is bound to.
- `~/.claude/projects/{slug}/{sid}.jsonl` (Claude Code's own transcript JSONL) — gives the Move-Zoom case where the orchestrator session itself ran the bash. Discovered via the M53b slug-decode helpers already in `scripts/hooks/gsd-t-conversation-capture.js`.

If the same activity appears in both readers, it dedupes by tool_use_id (which appears in both stream formats).

**R3 — Auto-switch policy.** When a new live entry appears in the rail, the bottom pane does NOT auto-switch to it. The entry pulses (CSS `@keyframes`, ~1.5s cycle, accent color) until the user either (a) clicks it, (b) it stops being live, or (c) 30 seconds elapse — whichever comes first. Pulse never persists past the entry's lifetime.

**R4 — Click behavior.** Clicking a live activity entry loads the bottom pane with that activity's live tail:
- Bash background command → tail of stdout/stderr (server endpoint reads `/proc/{pid}/fd/1` or the tee'd log file the existing `Bash run_in_background` writer produces).
- Monitor → the monitor stream (already exists internally; needs an HTTP surface).
- Tool use → the conversation frame for that tool, scrolled to and highlighted.
- Headless spawn → existing `/transcript/<spawn-id>` page (today's behavior).

**R5 — Visual.** New rail section header **"LIVE ACTIVITY"** between MAIN SESSION and LIVE SPAWNS. Each entry shows:
- Status dot (green=running, dimmed=stale-but-not-yet-removed)
- Activity type icon (`$` bash, `👁` monitor, `🔧` tool, `↳` spawn)
- One-line label (command name truncated to 40 chars)
- Wall-clock duration (live counter: `4m 12s`)
- Pulsing border for the first 30s of life or until clicked

**R6 — No effect on existing sections.** MAIN SESSION, LIVE SPAWNS, COMPLETED keep their current behavior. LIVE SPAWNS becomes a *sub-section* of LIVE ACTIVITY visually but its data source and existing journey specs stay untouched.

**R7 — Test against the live setup.** New `e2e/live-journeys/live-activity.spec.ts`:
- Start a real backgrounded bash sleep ⇒ entry appears in rail within 5s, pulses, shows duration ticking up.
- Stop the bash ⇒ entry disappears within 5s.
- Start two activities ⇒ both visible, both pulse.
- Click one ⇒ pulse stops on that one, bottom pane loads its tail.
- Self-skip when no dashboard reachable.

**R8 — Server-side surface.** New `GET /api/live-activity` endpoint, JSON shape:
```json
{ "schemaVersion": 1, "generatedAt": "...", "activities": [
  { "id": "...", "kind": "bash|monitor|tool|spawn", "label": "...",
    "startedAt": "...", "durationMs": 12345, "tailUrl": "/...", "alive": true }
] }
```
5-second cache (matches `/api/parallelism`). Pure read-only. Silent-fail invariant per the existing parallelism-report.cjs pattern.

## Scope this is NOT

- **Cross-project aggregation** (your Option A from earlier) — explicitly deferred. The new section reads only this dashboard's project + the orchestrator's own JSONL. We can come back to Option A later as its own milestone.
- **Renaming or removing existing sections.**
- **Changing the splitter, fullscreen toggle, or lazy-load** from your earlier message — those are 3 separate items, tracked separately, not bundled into this milestone.

## Where this lives

This is a **new milestone**, not a quick fix. The 4 prior items from your earlier message ("color match", "always-visible panes", "splitter drag", "fullscreen toggle", "lazy-load") are 4 more items still on the queue. This one (LIVE ACTIVITY) is the most-painful gap by your own words ("I am exhausted trying to get this to work"), so I want to ship it first as **M54 — Live Activity Visibility**.

## Question before I begin

**Sign-off check** — does R1–R8 read correctly? Specifically the auto-switch-NO + pulse-YES behavior in R3, and the bash-tail-via-PID approach in R4 (we'd need to verify Claude Code stores backgrounded-bash output where the dashboard server can reach it — that's the one technical risk).

If yes, say "go" and I'll:
1. Open M54, partition into 2 file-disjoint domains (D1: server endpoint + activity detector; D2: rail rendering + spec).
2. Write the contract.
3. Wave-execute end-to-end with Red Team + live-setup Playwright.
4. Ship as v3.24.10 (minor bump — new feature).

If you want changes, give me the diff.

Thu: May 7, 2026 14:58 PDT — GSD-T v3.23.11 — CURRENT

Going back to the lazy-load question from earlier. With your priority restated as **"page loads fast first, more loads on demand,"** here's my recommendation in plain terms:

## Recommendation: **Option 3 (mount-tail-of-prefetch)**

What it does:
1. **On open, fetch only the last 100 frames** from the server (new `?from=-100` query on the existing `/transcript/<id>` endpoint).
2. Page renders in <200 ms because we're parsing + DOM-mounting ~100 small NDJSON lines, not 10,000.
3. **Auto-scroll to bottom** (which is also the only thing on screen — the latest frames).
4. **Live SSE keeps appending** to the bottom as new frames arrive (existing behavior).
5. **As user scrolls toward the top**, an `IntersectionObserver` on the topmost frame fires and the client requests the next 100 frames before that index — **lazy load on demand**, no virtualization gymnastics, no upfront full-fetch.
6. Once the user has scrolled all the way back, the full transcript is in memory just like today, but they got there interactively, not at page-load.

## Why this and not the alternatives

| Option | Initial fetch | Initial render | Scroll-back | Server change | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Tail-only API + on-demand from server** ⭐ (this one) | 100 frames | <200ms | Streamed in 100-frame batches as you scroll up | Small (`?from=` param) | Matches your stated priority exactly |
| 2. Full fetch + DOM virtualization | All frames | 1-3s parse + 100ms render | Instant, but JS complexity high | None | Doesn't fix slow load — that's your main complaint |
| 3. Full fetch + mount-tail-only | All frames | Same slow fetch + parse | Mount-from-memory is instant | None | Initial load still slow because the full file is fetched |

Option 1 is the only one that addresses your **"page loads fast first"** requirement. Options 2 and 3 still pay the full-fetch cost upfront — they just hide it from the DOM. You said the slowness was the problem, so the fetch itself has to shrink.

## Edge cases I'd handle

- **Transcript shorter than 100 frames** → just return all of them, no scroll-back UI shown.
- **User scrolls down past the live tail then back up** → no double-load; we track the highest-index loaded.
- **SSE delivers a frame for a session whose `?from=-100` is already paged-back** → append at bottom as today; the in-memory frame index stays consistent.
- **Long bash output / huge tool_use payloads** → the 100-frame slice keeps render fast; one really big frame (say a 50KB tool_result) still renders in one go because that's atomic. Per-frame collapsing of payloads >2KB is an existing pattern in `gsd-t-transcript.html` — I'd reuse it.

## What it does NOT solve

- **Search across the whole transcript** — if you Ctrl-F before scrolling back, only the loaded 100 frames are searched. Pragmatic answer: a "Load all" button on the rail header that does the full prefetch on demand. Cheap to add, cheap to skip.

## Bundling with M54

This (**M55 — Lazy Transcript Load**) and the LIVE ACTIVITY (M54) work on the same file (`scripts/gsd-t-transcript.html`), so they will conflict if I run them in parallel. Two options:

- **Sequential**: M54 first, then M55. Clean. ~1.5x the wall-clock time.
- **Bundle**: M55 work piggybacks on M54's wave as a 3rd file-coordinated domain. Faster, more risk, harder review.

I lean **sequential** because M54 is the higher-value change and M55 needs server-side surgery on `handleTranscriptPage` that I want fully understood before touching.
